coredata.dc:title: A label-free fluorescence aptasensor for salivary exosomes based on a nano-micro dual-scale signal amplification strategy

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lu-Shuang
Chen, Xiao-Hao
Pei, Yu
Xie, Xiao-Lin
Wu, Cong-Ting
Cai, Shuang
Li, Jing
Gong, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is the most prevalent histological subtype of oral cancer and urgently requires a noninvasive approach for timely detection to improve patients’ prognose. Salivary exosome act as a promising biomarker, while the complex and expensive analytical methods impeded its popularization in clinical applications. In this study, we developed a label-free aptasensor for salivary exosomes that utilizes a “one exosome to multiple carbon dots” signal conversion method along with nano-micro dual-scale signal amplification strategy. SiO2 microspheres served as substrates, while carbon dots acted as luminophores, with CD63 aptamers functioning as selective recognition elements. Exosomes compete to bind with the aptamers, thereby leading to the release of non-specifically adsorbed carbon dots from the substrates. The concentration of exosomes is proportional to the variation in fluorescence intensity, enabling highly sensitive detection in artificial saliva samples, with a favorable linearity range of 2.5 × 102∼5 × 108 particles/mL and a relatively low LOD of 100 particles/mL. The anti-interference ability is deemed acceptable, and the fabrication process is affordable coupled with a straightforward signal output mode. The aptasensor was applied successfully to distinguish OSCC patients from healthy individuals, suggesting its potential for screening OSCC in physical examination centers and communities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Exosomes
Aptasensor
Carbon dots
Signal amplification
Label-free
Oral squamous cell carcinoma

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Exosomes sensing procedure and performance evaluation
Working and signal amplification mechanisms of the sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Discipline Construction Fund of Xiangyang Central Hospital

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this Research of Xiangyang Central Hospital Scientific Project (2024YJ04A), the Discipline Construction Fund of Xiangyang Central Hospital, and the Research Fund from the Collaborative Innovation Center of One Health of Hainan University (XTCX2022JKB05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the signal transformation and amplification strategies of the proposed aptasensor for salivary exosomes (PSDs come from Figdraw.com).

Characterization of CDs. (A) TEM image and size distribution of CDs-0; (B) TEM image and size distribution of CDs-1; (C) FTIR spectra; (D) XPS full scan survey; high resolution C1s (E), O1s (F), N1s (G) and (H) S 2p XPS spectra.

Characterization of model exosomes from CAL-27 cells. (A) TEM images (inset: magnification of a single exosome); (B) size distribution of model exosomes through NTA (inset: picture of exosomes captured in the analysis process); (C) immunoblotting strip images of proteins expressed in exosomes with cell lysate as control; (D ∼ G) identification of the exosomal membrane proteins through nFCM.

Characterization of the SiO2 substrates and the construction process of the SiO2-Apt-CDs. (A) SEM and TEM images of cSiO2; (B) SEM and TEM images of hSiO2; (C) nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm for cSiO2 (inset: pore-size distribution); (D) mercury intrusion-extrusion curve for hSiO2 (inset: pore-size distribution); FTIR spectra (E), Zeta potential (F) and TGA curves (G) of SiO2, SiO2–NH2, SiO2-Apt and SiO2-Apt-CDs; (H) confocal microscopy images of SiO2-Apt and hSiO2-Apt-CDs.

Characterization of the sensing process and the quantitative analysis performance of the two sensing systems. (Ã B) TEM images of the SiO2-Apt-CDs before and after the sensing incubation; (C) size distribution of model exosomes through NTA before and after sensing (inset: UV irradiation images of the SiO2-Apt-CDs before and after sensing); (D) correlation of variation in fluorescence intensity (ΔFL) with exosome concentrations (inset: linear fitting analysis of ΔFL versus logarithm of exosome concentrations).

Application performance of the proposed sensor based on hSiO2-Apt-CDs in artificial saliva samples and clinical patients. (A) specificity and anti-interference ability; (B) ΔFL of the supernatant in exosomes samples prepared with different artificial saliva ratios; (C) fluorescence spectra of the supernatant after sensing at varying exosomes concentrations in 100 % artificial saliva matrix; (D) correlation ofΔFL with exosome concentrations in 100 % artificial saliva matrix (inset: linear fitting analysis of ΔFL versus logarithm of exosome concentrations); (E) detected concentrations of salivary exosomes in seven healthy donors and seven OSCC patients; (F) comparison of the detected concentration of salivary exosomes in OSCC patients and healthy donors through the proposed aptasensor and NTA.
Comparison of the proposed aptasensor with other fluorescence sensors for exosomes in detection performance.

row.entry: DNA motor-driven endonuclease cleavage substrate and release FAM
TdT-mediated polymerization to form G-quadruplex and binding with ThT
CuO NPs transfer to Cu2+then to fluorescent CuNPs
branched rolling circle amplification of nucleic acid
CD63, AFP, CEA, EpCAM, PTK-7, PSMA, PDGF
DNase I digest the exposed aptamer from exosome and enable marker recycling to amplify recognition

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All the units have been converted into Particles/mL for ease of comparison with other reports.

head.ce:title: A label-free fluorescence aptasensor for salivary exosomes based on a nano-micro dual-scale signal amplification strategy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Lu-Shuang Li and Xiao-Hao Chen contributed this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is the most prevalent histological subtype of oral cancer and urgently requires a noninvasive approach for timely detection to improve patients’ prognose. Salivary exosome act as a promising biomarker, while the complex and expensive analytical methods impeded its popularization in clinical applications. In this study, we developed a label-free aptasensor for salivary exosomes that utilizes a “one exosome to multiple carbon dots” signal conversion method along with nano-micro dual-scale signal amplification strategy. SiO2 microspheres served as substrates, while carbon dots acted as luminophores, with CD63 aptamers functioning as selective recognition elements. Exosomes compete to bind with the aptamers, thereby leading to the release of non-specifically adsorbed carbon dots from the substrates. The concentration of exosomes is proportional to the variation in fluorescence intensity, enabling highly sensitive detection in artificial saliva samples, with a favorable linearity range of 2.5 × 102∼5 × 108 particles/mL and a relatively low LOD of 100 particles/mL. The anti-interference ability is deemed acceptable, and the fabrication process is affordable coupled with a straightforward signal output mode. The aptasensor was applied successfully to distinguish OSCC patients from healthy individuals, suggesting its potential for screening OSCC in physical examination centers and communities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is the most prevalent histological subtype of oral cancer, accounting for approximately 90 % of all cases. Timely detection of early-stage cancer can significantly reduce mortality rates and improve patients’ prognose [1]. Consequently, the pursuit of a noninvasive diagnostic approach for OSCC in clinical settings is of paramount importance. Exosomes, which are nanosized (30–150 nm) membrane vesicles secreted by cells and present in various bodily fluids, carry proteins, nucleic acids, lipids, and other biomolecules [2]. Recent research has identified exosomes as reliable biomarkers for early cancer detection [3,4]. Studies indicated that salivary exosome levels are significantly elevated in OSCC patients, correlating with disease progression, thus suggesting the potential of salivary exosomes as a promising liquid biopsy biomarker for OSCC [5,6]. A clinically applicable detection method is essential to facilitate the integration of salivary exosomes into routine clinical diagnosis.

Conventional methods for exosome quantification in laboratories, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, western blotting, nanoparticle tracking analysis, and flow cytometry, often encounter limitations in clinical settings due to high costs, low sensitivity, large sample requirements, and inadequate selectivity and anti-interference capabilities [7]. Aptamers, functioning as recognition elements, exhibit not only a strong affinity for their targets but also several advantages over antibodies, including a smaller size (2–3 nm), enhanced stability and flexibility, and a cost-effective and programmable synthesis process [8,9]. These characteristics render aptamers ideal ligands for exosome recognition. In recent years, aptamer-based sensing platforms (aptasensors) for exosomes have proliferated, coupling with various techniques such as fluorescence, surface plasmon resonance, electrochemistry, and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [10,11]. Despite these impressive advancements, the widespread clinical application of these sensors is still hindered by challenges such as complex and time-consuming device fabrication processes and the requirement for expensive equipment. Among these techniques, fluorescent aptasensors stand out for their ease of production and operation, affordability, and the availability of necessary instruments, making them promising and practical options for detection of exosomes.

In aptasensors for exosomes, aptamer targeting CD63 is commonly employed as the selective recognition element to achieve the affinity binding through the interaction between aptamers and CD63, which is a typical tetraspanin enriched on exosomes [11–15]. Consequently, current research efforts are primarily focused on enhancing the sensor's detection sensitivity. Various approaches have been explored, including branched rolling circle amplification [16], toehold-mediated DNA strand displacement [17], transform of CuO nanoparticles to Cu nanoparticles [15], and formation of G-quadruplex [14], among others [13,18,19]. While these methods provide signal amplification, they necessitate specialized designs, specific reagents, and complex procedures, which can be costly and time-consuming. Therefore, there is a pressing need to investigate novel signal amplification strategies and simpler detection methods. Carbon dots (CDs) are recognized as an optimal source of fluorescence signals and serve as excellent candidates for the construction of fluorescence sensors. This recognition arises from their advantages over traditional fluorescent agents, which include efficient luminescence, strong photostability, tunable excitation and emission spectra, non-toxicity, ease of synthesis, and eco-friendliness [20,21]. Although numerous studies have employed CDs for developing sensors aimed at detecting ions and small or large molecule biomarkers in vitro or in vivo [21–24], there has been limited research focusing on their application in exosome fluorescence detection.

In this study, we developed a label-free aptasensor that utilizes a “one exosome to multiple CDs” label-free signal conversion method along with nano-micro dual-scale signal amplification strategies for the highly sensitive detection of salivary exosomes. SiO2 microspheres served as substrates, while CDs acted as luminophores, with CD63 aptamers functioning as selective recognition elements. Exosomes compete to bind with the aptamers, thereby leading to the release of non-specifically adsorbed CDs from the SiO2, allowing for quantification of exosomes through variations in fluorescence intensity. Formaldehyde co-doping was employed to enhance the quantum yield of CDs, thereby increasing detection sensitivity at the nanoscale. Furthermore, the rough surface and micron-sized penetrable pores of the homemade hierarchically meso- and macro-porous SiO2 [25] facilitate more access for vesicular exosomes to aptamers, thus enhancing sensitivity at the microscale. The linearity and sensitivity of the aptasensor are favorable, coupled with affordable fabrication process. Additionally, the signal reading is straightforward, suggesting promising applications for screening OSCC patients in physical examination centers and communities.

Methanol, toluene, hydrochloric acid (HCl), formaldehyde (HCHO, 37 %), and thiourea of analytical grade were procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-Ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was sourced from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), glycine (Gly), L-glutamic acid (Glu), L-cysteine (Cys), L-tryptophan (Trp), catalase (CAT) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were acquired from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Lysozyme and dialysis bags (1000 MWCO) were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Human immunoglobulin G (IgG) and artificial saliva were bought from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The CAL-27 cell line was delivered from Wuhan Pricella Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). High-glucose DMEM medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.2) were purchased from Gibco (Thermo, USA). Commercial mesoporous silica microspheres (cSiO2) were procured from Bangkai High-Tech Materials Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Homemade hierarchically porous silica microspheres were synthesized as our previous report. The carboxyl-modified CD63 aptamer (AptCD63) was synthesized and delivered by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) with the sequence (5′ to 3′) being CAC CCC ACC TCG CTC CCG TGA CAC TAA TGC TA. Ultrapure water was prepared by an LUE-20 water purification system (HyperPureX, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained using a Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescence studies were performed on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Nitrogen adsorption isotherms and mercury intrusion for porosity characterization were conducted on a TriStar II Plus surface area analyzer and an AutoPore V 9600 mercury porosimetry instrument (Micromeritics, USA), respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on a TG 209 F3 Tarsus instrument (Netzsch, Germany). An FV3000 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Olympus, Japan) and a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 potentiometer (Malvern Panalytical, UK) were utilized to observe fluorescence and measure the potential of the materials, respectively.

The isolation of exosomes was performed using a Multifuge X1R centrifuge (Thermo Fisher, USA) and an Optima XE-100 ultracentrifuge (Beckman Coulter, USA), equipped with Ti70 and SW41 rotors. The characterization of the particle size and concentration of the isolated exosomes was conducted using a ZetaView PMX120 Nanoparticle Tracking Analyzer (NTA, Particle Metrix, Germany) and a Dimension ICON Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, Bruker, USA). Following negative staining with phosphotungstic acid, the morphology of the exosomes was examined using an HT7700 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi, Japan). The exosomal proteins were characterized through Western blotting and an N30E nano flow analyzer (nFCM, NanoFCM Inc, Xiamen, China).

For the preparation of CDs-0, a solution was created by dissolving 1.0 g of citric acid and 0.02 g of thiourea in 10 mL of ultrapure water. This precursor solution was then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting products were collected, filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon filter, and dialyzed against ultrapure water for 48 h using a dialysis tube with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000. The dialysate was subsequently vacuum evaporated and dried to obtain the solid material for further use. In the case of CDs-1, 0.2 mL of formaldehyde (HCHO) solution was intentionally added to the precursor solution.

The optimization of preparation conditions for the CDs involved a systematic study of the doping amounts of thiourea (0∼0.2 g) and HCHO (0∼1.0 mL), as well as variations in hydrothermal temperature (140∼220 °C) and time duration (3∼12 h). The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs served as the optimization indicator and was measured with quinine sulfate as a reference (QY: 54 % at Ex of 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4).

The UV–Vis spectra, along with the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs prepared under optimal conditions, were recorded to elucidate their essential optical properties. Additionally, the fluorescence stability of the CDs was investigated in terms of pH (3–12), ionic strength (0∼1.0 M NaCl) and photobleaching (exposed to continuous irradiation for 1 h).

The cSiO2 and hSiO2 were refluxed in a 6 M HCl solution at a ratio of 1 g–10 mL at 90 °C for 12 h respectively to activate the silanol groups. After washing to neutral and drying under vacuum, 1.0 g of the material was devoted to 10 mL of toluene containing 0.5 mL of APTES and treated hydrothermally at 110 °C for 8 h to facilitate the bonding of amino groups. The obtained products (NH2–SiO2) were washed successively with toluene and ethanol, followed by drying for backup.

To modify AptCD63 on NH2–SiO2, the COOH-modified AptCD63 was dissolved in PBS to achieve a concentration of 100 μM. Initially, 360 μL of the aptamer solution was heated to 95 °C for 10 min and then allowed to anneal to room temperature gradually. Subsequently, it was activated in 1.8 mL of EDC/NHS solution (400 mM/100 mM) for 0.5 h. Thereafter, 120 mg of NH2–SiO2 was added to the mixture and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, followed by washing with pure water for three times, resulting in the intermediate SiO2-Apt.

To introduce CDs onto SiO2-Apt via physical adsorption, 1 mL of CDs-1 solution (0.1 mg/mL) was added to the prepared SiO2-Apt and incubated overnight with continuous gentle shaking. The products were washed several times with water to remove any unbound CDs, yielding SiO2-Apt-CDs, which were then resuspended in 2.0 mL of PBS and vortexed thoroughly before use. The SiO2-Apt-CDs, prepared from cSiO2 and hSiO2, were designated as cSiO2-Apt-CDs and hSiO2-Apt-CDs, respectively.

CAL-27 cells were cultured in high-glucose DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % (v/v) FBS and 1 % (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. Once the cells reached 80 %∼90 % confluency, the medium was removed, and the cells were washed twice with PBS to eliminate dead cells. Then the cells were cultured in FBS-free medium for 24 h to stimulate exosome secretion in the serum starvation state. The supernatant of the culture medium was collected and subjected to differential centrifugation for exosome isolation, with all centrifugation steps performed at 4 °C. Firstly, it was centrifuged at 1000 g for 10 min to remove dead cells and then centrifuged at 3000 g for 15 min to remove cell debris and apoptotic bodies. Subsequently, the resulting supernatant was centrifuged at 10000 g for 30 min to remove larger granular vesicles. The resulting supernatant was then centrifuged at 120, 000 g for 2 h to acquire exosomes. Finally, the exosomes in different centrifuge bottles were resuspended with PBS and merged into a single bottle, followed by an additional centrifugation at 120,000 g for another 2 h to harvest pure exosomes. The final precipitate was resuspended in PBS to generate the primary exosome suspension.

The morphology of the obtained exosomes was observed by TEM after negative staining for 3 min with 1% phosphotungstic acid. The size distribution and concentration of the exosomes were determined through NTA after diluting the sample with PBS to an appropriate concentration. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) was employed to further characterize the size distribution of the exosomes. Western blotting was performed to verify the protein markers of exosomes. Specifically, exosome lysate was loaded into each well and separated by SDS-PAGE. The separated proteins were then transferred onto a polyvinylidene fluoride membrane. After blocking with 5% skim milk, the membrane was incubated overnight at 4°C with primary antibodies (anti-CD63 (25682-1-AP, Proteintech), anti-CD9 (25597-1-AP, Proteintech), anti-TSG101 (ab125011, Abcam), anti-HSP70 (10995-1-AP) and anti-Calnexin (ab22595, Abcam)). Following this, it was washed and incubated at room temperature for 1 h with HRP-labeled secondary antibodies (SA00001-1 and SA00001-2, Proteintech). Finally, the targeted proteins were visualized using an enhanced ECL kit and detected with an Amersham ImageQuant 800 Imaging System (Cytiva). The membrane proteins on the surface of exosomes were further analyzed through nFCM. Specifically, after diluting the exosomes with PBS, fluorescently labeled antibodies against CD9, CD63, and CD81 (555371, 557288, and 551108, BD Biosciences) were added respectively and incubated at 37°C for 30 min in the dark. Subsequently, 1 mL of pre-chilled PBS was added, and the mixture was ultracentrifuged at 110,000×g for 70 min at 4°C to remove excess fluorescent antibodies. The resulting product was washed with PBS and subjected to another round of ultracentrifugation under the same conditions and resuspended in PBS before analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exosomes sensing procedure and performance evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical sensing procedure for exosomes, 50 μL of the SiO2-Apt-CDs suspension (60 μg/μL) was added to 200 μL of exosomes at varying concentration levels (2.5 × 102∼5 × 108 particles/mL in PBS). The mixture was vortexed homogeneously for 1 min and then incubated for 0.5 h at room temperature with gentle shaking. Upon completion, the system was centrifuged, and the fluorescence spectrum of the supernatant was measured at an Ex of 345 nm, with the fluorescence intensity (F) recorded at Em of 425 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the blank system (F0) was acquired by substituting the exosomes with PBS. The variation in fluorescence intensity (ΔFL, F–F0) was calculated, and linear fitting analysis with the logarithm of exosome concentrations (lg [Exosomes]) was performed. The limit of detection was calculated at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Additionally, the performance of sensing systems based on cSiO2-derived and hSiO2-derived materials was compared.
For optimization of sensing conditions, the concentration of SiO2-Apt-CDs (10∼75 μg/μL), incubation time (10∼90 min) and temperature (4∼45°C) were investigated respectively.

The selectivity of the system was investigated when the exosomes suspension (5 × 105 particles/mL) was replaced with IgG (2 mg/mL), lysozyme (Lys, 2 mg/L), CAT (100 U/mL), BSA (2 mg/mL) and amino acids solutions (Gly, Glu, Cys and Trp, 2 mg/mL) respectively. To further assess the applicability of the sensing system in reality, artificial saliva was spiked into the PBS for exosome dispersion at different ratios (10∼100%). The ΔFL value of the sensing system was compared with the case of 100 % PBS. Moreover, 100% artificial saliva spiked with exosomes at different concentrations (2.5 × 102∼5 × 108 particles/mL) were analyzed through the hSiO2-Apt-CDs-based sensing system at the optimal conditions. The fluorescence intensity variation (ΔFL) was linear fitted with lg [Exosomes].

Saliva samples from OSCC patients and healthy donors were obtained from Xiangyang Central Hospital, with approval from the institution's Ethics Committees. The clinical information of the patients is presented in Table S1. The collected fresh saliva samples were first filtered through a nylon cell strainer (70 μm) and then centrifuged at 3000 g for 30 min, 10000 g for 30 min, and 120000 g for 2 h, successively. All the centrifugation steps were performed at 4°C. The obtained exosomes were resuspended in 100 μL of PBS and the analyzed through the proposed sensor based on hSiO2-Apt-CDs under optimal conditions with a dilution factor of 2000. The significance of the differences between healthy donors and OSCC patients was assessed through an unpaired t-test at a 95% confidence interval with Prism 8.0 software. The results were also compared with those detected through NTA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working and signal amplification mechanisms of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: This study presents the development of a label-free fluorescence sensor for exosome detection, utilizing SiO2 as the substrate and CDs as the fluorescence signal. The signal transformation and amplification strategies of the sensor are depicted in Fig. 1 . Initially, SiO2 was reacted with APTES to produce SiO2–NH2. Subsequently, the carboxyl group-modified CD63 aptamer (Apt) was immobilized on SiO2–NH2 through EDC/NHS cross-linking reaction, resulting in SiO2-Apt. The SiO2-Apt was then exposed to CDs solution. The CDs were non-specifically adsorbed onto the inner and outer surfaces of SiO2-Apt via non-covalent interactions, including hydrophobic, electrostatic, and hydrogen bonding interactions, culminating in the formation of the final sensor, SiO2-Apt-CDs. For the sensing procedure, SiO2-Apt-CDs were introduced into the sample solution containing exosomes. The Apt on SiO2 selectively combined with the exosomal membrane proteins (CD63), thereby capturing the exosomes. The relatively weaker interactions between Apt and CDs were broken, resulting in the release of CDs into the solution. Following centrifugation to remove the SiO2-Apt-Exos, the fluorescence intensity of the resulting supernatant containing the CDs was measured. To enhance detection sensitivity, this study proposed a dual-scale signal amplification strategy.

On one hand, the concentration of CDs in the solution is directly proportional to the number of exosomes present in the sample, thus realizing the quantification of exosomes through fluorescence intensity measurements and signal amplification through a “one exosome to multiple CDs” strategy. Consequently, improving the quantum yield could augment the signal output of individual CDs, thereby increasing detection sensitivity in appearance (nanoscale). In this study, the hydrothermal method was utilized to synthesize CDs, employing citric acid as the primary carbon source and thiourea as a heteroatom dopant. Additionally, formaldehyde co-doping was explored to further enhance the quantum yield of the CDs as our previous work.

On the other hand, despite the abundance of CD63 on the exosome membrane, the use of a conventional commercial mesoporous SiO2 microsphere (cSiO2) as a substrate resulted in each exosome primarily contacting the substrate tangentially, thereby binding less aptamers due to steric hindrance. This limitation led to a restricted number of CDs being released, as shown in Path (a) in Fig. 1. Consequently, we considered substituting cSiO2 with our own developed homemade hierarchically meso- and macro-porous SiO2 (hSiO2) to enhance sensitivity inherently (microscale). As displayed in Path (b) in Fig. 1, the rough surface of hSiO2 is conducive to the access for vesicular exosomes to more aptamers within the same contact area. Additionally, the micron-sized penetrable pores in hSiO2 enable exosomes to enter the matrix, which is unprocurable in mesoporous cSiO2. That is to say, the CDs inside the microspheres could not be substituted by exosomes. In the two aspects, the substituted CDs from hSiO2-Apt based system should be more than those from cSiO2-Apt based system, thus resulting a stronger response to exosomes theoretically. The sensing performance of the two matrixes will be investigated detailly in the later section.

By incorporating the “one exosome to multiple CDs” label-free signal transformation strategy with the dual-scale signal amplification effects, we anticipate a significant enhancement in the sensor's detection sensitivity for exosomes.

In this study, the hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize CDs using citric acid and thiourea, both of which have been extensively validated as effective carbon sources and heteroatom dopants in our previous research [26]. Building on our earlier findings, formaldehyde was introduced as a co-dopant in the reaction system to further enhance the quantum yield of the CDs [27]. Key reaction conditions affecting the formation of CDs were optimized, specifically the amounts of thiourea and formaldehyde dopants, as well as the reaction temperature and duration, utilizing a single-variable approach (Fig. S1). The optimal synthesis conditions are detailed in Section 2.2. As anticipated, the incorporation of formaldehyde significantly increased the quantum yield of the CDs, with the yield rising from 25 % to 45 % when compared to CDs doped solely with thiourea. The fluorescence mechanisms of CDs primarily involve surface defect emission, luminescence from surface fluorescent groups, and the quantum size confinement effect, all of which are significantly influenced by the size and functional group composition of the CDs [28–30].

To further clarify the mechanism by which formaldehyde enhances the quantum yield of CDs, a comparative characterization was performed between CDs co-doped with formaldehyde (CDs-1) and those without formaldehyde co-doping (CDs-0). Both CDs-0 and CDs-1 exhibited quasi-spherical shapes and were well dispersed, as illustrated in TEM images (Fig. 2A–B). Statistical analysis indicated that the sizes of CDs-0 and CDs-1 were 3.58 ± 0.5 nm and 2.96 ± 0.4 nm, respectively. The particle size of CDs-1 is smaller than that of CDs-0, suggesting that the observed increase in quantum yield may be influenced by quantum confinement effect [29,31]. The doping of formaldehyde may result in a reduction of particle size for the CDs. The elemental and functional group compositions of CDs-0 and CDs-1 were analyzed using FTIR and XPS techniques. As shown in Fig. 2C, both CDs-0 and CDs-1 exhibited similar FTIR spectra. A broad absorption band at 3110 cm⁻1 was attributed to O–H/N–H stretching vibrations. Furthermore, absorption peaks at 1709 cm⁻1, 1408 cm⁻1 and 1309 cm⁻1 indicated the presence of CO, C–N, and COO− groups, respectively. The signal around 1034 cm−1 and 627 cm−1 may be attributed to and CS, SO and C–S functional groups, respectively [32,33]. Given the limited information provided by FTIR, a further examination using XPS was conducted. The full XPS scan spectra (Fig. 2D) revealed the presence of C 1s (299 eV), O 1s (546 eV), N 1s (411 eV), and S 2p (176 eV) in both CDs, with varying composition ratios. Specifically, the high-resolution C 1s spectra of CDs-0 and CDs-1 exhibited three peaks at 284 eV, 286 eV, and 288 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O/C–S/C–N, and CO, respectively (Fig. 2E). Compared to CDs-0, CDs-1 displayed a higher proportion of C–O/C–S/C–N. The high-resolution O 1s spectra of both CDs showed two signals at 531 eV and 532 eV, attributed to CO and C–O, respectively (Fig. 2F). Additionally, the high-resolution N 1s spectra indicated that both CDs contained pyridinic N at 399 eV, pyrrolic N at 400 eV, and graphitic N at 401 eV, with CDs-1 having a greater relative amount of C–N–C and a lesser amount of N–H than CDs-0 (Fig. 2G) [34]. Moreover, the high-resolution S 2p spectra indicated the presence of phenyl-SO3 (168 eV) and sulfide (C–S–C, 163 eV) in both CDs (Fig. 2H) [26], with CDs-1 exhibiting a higher relative amount of C–S–C than CDs-0. Overall, the XPS results revealed a higher content of C–N–C and C–S–C groups in CDs-1 compared to CDs-0. It is suggested that the increased content of C–N–C and C–S–C groups in CDs-1 may impact surface defect states and facilitate the formation of a conjugated π electron system in the carbon backbone, potentially influencing electron-hole recombination [35]. These findings indicate that formaldehyde co-doping may enhance quantum yield by reducing particle size and modifying the defects and functional groups on the surface. Hence, CDs-1 were adopted in the subsequent experiments. The Optical properties and stability of CDs were displayed in Fig. S2. The optimal excitation wavelength (Ex) and emission wavelength (Em) of CDs-1 were 345 nm and 430 nm, respectively.

CAL-27 cell-derived exosomes were adopted as the model exosomes in this work and were isolated from the culture medium supernatant through ultracentrifugation. The obtained exosomes were characterized through TEM, NTA and western blotting respectively. As observed in the TEM image (Fig. 3 A), the exosomes showed a typical cup-shaped morphology, and the structure of the membrane vesicles was clearly defined. The primary exosome suspension was diluted to an appropriate concentration prior to NTA analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the average hydrodynamic diameter of the exosomes was measured at 127.6 nm, which aligns with the literature [12]. The concentration of the primary exosome suspension was calculated to be 3.2 × 1011 particles/mL. The AFM images of the exosomes, displayed in Fig. S3, provided supporting evidence of exosome sizes ranging from tens to hundreds of nanometers. In accordance with the guidelines of MISEV 2018 [36], the protein markers of the isolated exosomes were characterized through western blotting, with the parent cell lysate as a control. The immunoblotting strip images were displayed in Fig. 3C. CD63, CD9, TSG101, and HSP70 were all expressed in the exosomes, while calnexin, as the negative control, was only detectable in the cell lysate, demonstrating the presence of exosomes with favorable purity. The exosomal membrane proteins were labeled with fluorescent antibodies and sorted through a nFCM. The results are presented in Fig. 3(D∼G). Comparing with control, the positive rates for CD9, CD63, and CD81 were satisfactory, further confirming the exosomes used in this study were accurately identified. Meanwhile, the working principle of the constructed sensor, which is based on the specific binding interaction between the anchored CD63 and AptCD63, is deemed feasible.

SEM, TEM, nitrogen adsorption, and mercury compression techniques were employed to investigate the differences in surface structures and pore sizes between cSiO2 and hSiO2. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A∼4B, both materials are mono-dispersed microspheres with particle sizes approximately ranging from 4 to 6 μm. The TEM images included as insets in Fig. 4A and 4B, clearly demonstrated that both materials exhibit a mesoporous structure with a nanometer-sized pore diameter, which is consistent with the BET results shown in Fig. 4C. Fig. 4C∼4D indicated that the average pore sizes of cSiO2 and hSiO2 are estimated to be 15 nm and 1 μm, respectively. Due to its smooth surface and smaller pore size, cSiO2 can only interact with exosomes at tangent points. In contrast, the presence of both mesopores and macropores confirms the hierarchically porous structure of hSiO2, which provides a high surface area and facilitates access for exosomes concurrently. Additionally, the gully-like concave and convex structures, along with the macropores of hSiO2, create additional spaces for interaction with exosomes, serving as the material basis for signal amplification. Since the modification process is unaffected by pore structure, only hSiO2 was utilized to characterize the modification process, hereafter referred to as SiO2.

FTIR, zeta potential, and TGA were employed to confirm the successful modification of aptamers on SiO2. As illustrated in Fig. 4E, the FTIR spectrum of SiO2 displays characteristic peaks at 1097 cm⁻1 and 809 cm⁻1, corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of Si–O–Si and the Si–OH stretching vibrations, respectively. The stretching vibration peak at 3460 cm⁻1 is attributed to the O–H and N–H groups [25]. After aptamer functionalization, a new peak appears at 1687 cm⁻1 in the FTIR spectrum of SiO2-Apt (pink line), attributed to the stretching vibration of CN in imine, indicating successful aptamer functionalization [37]. As shown in Fig. 4F, the zeta potential of SiO2–NH2 increased from −21.2 mV to 15.3 mV compared to SiO2, confirming the successful modification with amino groups. In contrast, the zeta potential of SiO2-Apt decreased from 15.3 mV to −6.7 mV, which can be attributed to the negative charge of the aptamer and the reduction in amino groups following their reaction with the aptamer. When the CDs were adsorbed and connected with the aptamer, the zeta potential turned from negative to positive again, rising to 16.9 mV [12]. This change in zeta potential provides confirmation that each step in the process is indeed occurring. TGA (Fig. 4G) revealed that the weight losses of SiO2, SiO2–NH2, SiO2-Apt, and SiO2-Apt-CDs, when subjected to air heating from 200 °C to 800 °C, were 1.69 %, 5.57 %, 9.12 % and 10.89 %, respectively. The incremental weight loss relative to SiO2 can be attributed to the oxidative decomposition of the incorporated organic groups, indicating successful modification with aptamers and the adsorption of CDs. Confocal microscopy was employed to further investigate the adsorption of CDs on SiO2-Apt. As illustrated in Fig. 4H, the size of the material remained largely consistent before and after the adsorption of CDs, and the dispersion was effectively maintained in a monodisperse state. SiO2-Apt-CDs exhibited a pronounced blue fluorescence signal upon excitation at 355 nm, whereas no fluorescence signal was detected in SiO2-Apt. This observation confirms the successful adsorption of CDs onto SiO2-Apt. Collectively, all the characterization results corroborate each other and together confirm that the SiO2-Apt-CDs was successfully prepared as envisaged.

According to the working principle, the sensor was developed through the process of SiO2-Apt-CDs efficiently capturing exosomes while simultaneously releasing CDs. TEM images of SiO2-Apt-CDs were obtained before and after the sensing incubation. As shown in Fig. 5 A∼5B, a significant number of morphologically intact exosomes were observed attached to the surface of SiO2-Apt-CDs post-incubation, confirming the effective capture of exosomes by the sensor. Furthermore, the concentrations of exosomes remaining in the supernatant after treatment with SiO2-Apt-CDs were analyzed using NTA. A marked decrease in exosome concentration following incubation with SiO2-Apt-CDs was observed (Fig. 5C), further affirming the high efficiency of these sensors in exosome capture. To assess whether SiO2-Apt-CDs can simultaneously release CDs upon exosome capture, the solid phase and supernatant after sensing were collected separately for fluorescence analysis. The collected solid phase was redispersed in PBS at the same concentration as the sensing system, and UV irradiation images were captured (insets in Fig. 5C). Compared to the sensing system before incubation with exosomes, SiO2-Apt-CDs exhibited a noticeable decrease in fluorescence intensity after sensing incubation. Moreover, following the sensing process, the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant gradually increased with rising exosome concentrations in the sample (Fig. S4(A-B)), confirming the release of CDs into the liquid sample matrix. These results provided strong evidence for the simultaneous release of CDs by SiO2-Apt-CDs upon exosome capture. In summary, we conclude that SiO2-Apt-CDs can efficiently capture exosomes while simultaneously releasing CDs, demonstrating the successful implementation of our designed sensing process for exosome detection.

The principle of the proposed sensing method for exosomes hinges on the interaction between exosomes and SiO2-Apt-CDs, which is essential for both the capture of exosomes and the release of CDs. The variation in fluorescence intensity (ΔFL) of the supernatant reflects the quantity of CDs substituted by exosomes. Thus, it is crucial to investigate the sensing conditions in detail, including the initial concentration of SiO2-Apt-CDs, incubation duration and temperature. As shown in Fig. S5A, ΔFL increased with the rising concentration of SiO2-Apt-CDs, reaching a peak at 60 μg/μL, which indicates that the interaction between exosomes and the aptamers approaches saturation. An appropriate extension of the incubation duration or increase in incubation temperature can facilitate the sensing procedure, as displayed in Fig. S5(B-C). However, excessively prolonged incubation time may introduce fluorescent background interference due to the shedding of non-substituted CDs. Similarly, inordinate high temperature can denature exosomal surface proteins, impeding the binding of exosomes to aptamers. Overall consideration including convenience, SiO2-Apt-CDs of 60 μg/μL, an incubation duration of 30 min, and room temperature were adopted as the optimal sensing conditions subsequently.

As clarified in Section 3.1, the proposed dual-scale signal amplification strategy for exosomes based on SiO2-Apt-CDs could enhance detection sensitivity signally. Moreover, the superiority of hierarchically meso- and macro-porous hSiO2 over mesoporous cSiO2 should be emphasized. Consequently, the quantitative analysis performance and the sensitivity of the two sensing systems based on hSiO2-Apt-CDs and cSiO2-Apt-CDs were inspected and compared. As observed in Fig. S4(A-B), the increasing concentration of exosomes lighted up the fluorescence emission spectrum of the supernatant in both sensing systems. Furthermore, the variation in fluorescence intensity (ΔFL) was calculated, displaying an upward tendency with the rising concentration gradient of exosomes in both systems (Fig. 5D). Linear correlation analysis of ΔFL versus the logarithm of exosome concentration in the two systems were performed severally, yielding the coefficients of determination (R2) of 0.9992 and 0.9799, which indicate favorable linearities for both (inset of Fig. 5D).

It is worth noting that the response gap between the two sensing systems to exosomes enlarged progressively with the increasing exosomes concentrations. Thus hSiO2-Apt-CDs revealed a relatively superior detection performance for exosomes. The linear ranges for the two systems were 2.5 × 102∼5 × 108 particles/mL for hSiO2-Apt-CDs and 5.0 × 102∼5 × 108 particles/mL for cSiO2-Apt-CDs. According to the 3δ/k rule, the limits of detection (LODs) were calculated to be 100 particles/mL for hSiO2-Apt-CDs and 250 particles/mL for cSiO2-Apt-CDs, respectively.

The hierarchically porous structure of hSiO2 is speculated to achieve the superiority. As depicted in Figs. 1, 4B and 4D, hSiO2 features a bumpy surface and micron-level tunnels, which are conducive to the access of vesicular exosomes to the aptamers. The rough surface allows for binding of one exosome to more aptamers within the same contact area. Whereas in the case of cSiO2, exosomes primarily contacted with the substrate tangentially. Moreover, the micron-sized penetrable pores in hSiO2 enable exosomes to enter the matrix, which is unprocurable in mesoporous cSiO2. From the two aspects, the substituted CDs from hSiO2-Apt-CDs systems were relatively more than those from cSiO2-Apt-CDs, thus resulting a stronger response to exosomes and enhanced sensitivity. This conclusion was further supported by the slightly steeper slope of the correlation equation for hSiO2-Apt-CDs compared to cSiO2-Apt-CDs. Deservedly, all subsequent experiments were conducted with hSiO2-Apt-CDs.

In comparison to previously reported fluorescence sensing systems for exosomes (Table 1 ), the proposed dual-scale signal amplification strategy generated a response model of “one exosome-numerous CDs” and led to satisfactory detection performance. It has comparable or broader linearity range and higher sensitivity, while also saving working time, thus paving a way for clinical applications.

To inspect the specificity and anti-interference ability, sensing procedures were executed with exosomes either replaced or mixed with substances commonly found in saliva, such as enzymes, amino acids, and proteins [38]. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the purple-grey bars reflect the sensor's favorable selectivity for exosomes. Exosomes at a relatively high concentration (5 × 105 particles/mL) elicited a remarkably stronger response. Furthermore, exosomes at the limit of quantitation (LOQ) concentration (5 × 102 particles/mL) also produced a minimum 2.5-fold higher ΔFL than the others. The orange bars represent the anti-interference ability, showing that and the impacts on sensing ranged from 60 % to 140 % when exosomes were present in trace amounts. Considering the high concentration of exosomes in real samples, this influence is considered negligible.

Additionally, exosomes diluted with a mixture of PBS and commercial artificial saliva were adopted as target for analysis to validate the applicability of the proposed sensing system in a practical matrix. As the proportion of artificial saliva increased, the resulting ΔFL initially decreased before stabilizing (Fig. 6B). The observed interference may be attributed that the substances in saliva could jointly interfere with the interaction between exosomes and aptamers. Quantitative determination of exosomes diluted with 100 % artificial saliva was conducted, with results displayed in Fig. 6C∼6D. A favorable linearity in the exosomes concentration ranges of 2.5 × 102∼5 × 108 particles/mL and a low LOD of 100 particles/mL were achieved. The slope of the correlation equation (327.45) was slightly lower than the case of PBS as the matrix (346.01), further implying the faint influence of the matrix on the sensing procedure. In general, the anti-interference ability is deemed acceptable, indicating the great potential of the sensor for practical applications.

It has been reported that the concentration of exosomes in the body fluids of cancer patients is significantly higher than that in healthy individuals, a trend that also applies to salivary exosomes in OSCC [5]. On basis of this, the proposed sensor may serve as an attractive tool to distinguish cancer patients and healthy individuals. Exosomes isolated from the saliva samples of ten healthy donors and ten OSCC patients were analyzed through the sensing system and NTA, respectively. The fluorescence response and calculated concentrations are enumerated in Table S2. Fig. 6E exhibits a significant difference in the quantity of saliva-derived exosomes between the two groups, analyzed through unpaired t-test with a P-value of <0.0001. In other words, OSCC group secret more exosomes into per unit volume of saliva, the trends observed are consistent with the NTA results shown in Fig. 6F. However, it is noteworthy that the concentrations obtained from NTA were relatively higher, potentially due to the inability to distinguish protein aggregates from exosomes [12].

Besides, CD63 was adopted as a model protein herein to construct a general and feasible method for detecting exosomes in clinical samples, which is more meaningful to high-volume screening in community-based health service centers or physical examination organizations. It can also provide important afflatus for the development of new liquid biopsy strategies aimed at screening for specific cancers when CD63 is replaced with other tumor-specific membrane proteins, such as MUC1, EpCAM, or HER-2.

In this study, we developed a label-free aptasensor that utilizes a “one exosome to multiple carbon dots” label-free signal conversion method along with nano-micro dual-scale signal amplification strategies. SiO2 microspheres served as substrates, while carbon dots acted as luminophores, with CD63 aptamers functioning as selective recognition elements. A favorable linearity range and a relatively low LOD were achieved in the artificial saliva samples, coupled with acceptable anti-interference ability, affordable fabrication process and straightforward signal-reading model. The aptasensor was capable of distinguish OSCC patients from healthy individuals, based on the difference in exosome expression of the two groups. It exhibited potential for screening OSCC in physical examination centers and communities and has the opportunity to be developed into an attractive tool for the simultaneous screening of multiple malignant tumors.

Lu-Shuang Li: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiao-Hao Chen: Methodology, Data curation. Yu Pei: Methodology. Xiao-Lin Xie: Investigation. Cong-Ting Wu: Software. Shuang Cai: Validation. Jing Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wei Gong: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this Research of Xiangyang Central Hospital Scientific Project (2024YJ04A), the Discipline Construction Fund of Xiangyang Central Hospital, and the Research Fund from the Collaborative Innovation Center of One Health of Hainan University (XTCX2022JKB05).
